23: Ephesus was the administrative capital of the Roman province of Asia (western Turkey) and home to the Artemis temple (one of the Seven Wonders of the ancient world; rebuilt by Croesus; the Ephesian Artemis was a fertility goddess distinct from the Greek Artemis). The silversmith Demetrius's complaint exposes the economic dimension of religious conflict: the naopouloi (temple-business workers) who made naoi (small silver shrines of Artemis) for pilgrims and votaries faced economic ruin if Ephesians converted. The Ephesian assembly (ekklesia) is an authentic Roman civic body — the use of the same word ekklesia for the Christian community was deliberately countercultural.
1: While Apollos was at Corinth, Paul passed through the inland country and came to Ephesus: the 'inland country' (ta anōterika merē) is the highland route through Phrygia and Galatia rather than the coastal road — consistent with the strengthening visits of Acts 18:23. Ephesus (modern Selçuk, Turkey) was the premier city of Roman Asia, population estimated at 200,000-500,000, with the Artemis temple as its defining monument and tourism industry. Paul's three-year Ephesian ministry (Acts 20:31) was the longest continuous urban mission in Acts.
2: Finding some disciples (mathētas) who had not heard there is a Holy Spirit: the dozen 'disciples' (Acts 19:7) in Ephesus parallel the Samaritan situation of Acts 8 — baptized believers without evident Spirit-reception. Their description as disciples of John (Acts 19:3) and their ignorance of the Spirit identifies them as the easternmost extension of the Baptist movement. John's ministry in Judea produced communities that spread through the diaspora without the Pentecost developments; these twelve represent one such pocket community.
3: 'Into what then were you baptized?' — Paul's diagnostic question reveals the theological gap: John's baptism was preparatory (for repentance, in anticipation of the Coming One) while Christian baptism is in the name of Jesus and carries Spirit-reception. The distinction between these two forms of initiation was important enough in the early church that Luke documents it three times: the Samaritan situation (Acts 8:14-17), Apollos (Acts 18:25-26), and these Ephesian twelve. The Spirit is the mark of the new era, not just a supplement to proper procedure.
4: 'John baptized with the baptism of repentance (baptisma metanoias), telling the people to believe in the one who was to come after him, that is, Jesus' — Paul's explanation grounds John's baptism as genuine but anticipatory. The Baptist himself had pointed to a successor ('one coming after me, the strap of whose sandals I am not worthy to untie'); his baptism was calibrated to that expectation. Those who had received John's baptism had been rightly oriented toward Jesus but had not yet encountered the fulfillment of what John promised.
5: On hearing this they were baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus (eis to onoma tou kyriou Iēsou): the rebaptism of those who had only John's baptism establishes that Christian baptism is distinct from and cannot be collapsed into John's. The 'name of the Lord Jesus' formula (used in Acts in several variations: Acts 2:38; Acts 8:16; Acts 10:48; Acts 19:5) identifies the mediating authority under which the baptism is performed — the risen Christ whose name is invoked. The immediate sequence of baptism then laying on of hands follows the Samaritan pattern.
6: When Paul had laid his hands on them, the Holy Spirit came on them (ēlthen to pneuma to hagion ep autous) and they began speaking in tongues and prophesying: the arrival of the Spirit with immediate charismata mirrors Pentecost (Acts 2) and Cornelius's household (Acts 10:44-46). The consistent pattern across these boundary-crossing moments — Spirit arriving with visible/audible manifestation — is Luke's theological signature: each major expansion of the community has its own miniature Pentecost authenticating the inclusion. The twelve Ephesians are the last such group in Acts.
7: There were about twelve men in all (hōsei andres dōdeka): the twelve is likely not accidental — the number echoes the Twelve apostles and symbolically represents a restored Israel community. The Ephesian twelve receive the Spirit and begin the community that will become one of the most significant in early Christianity (the Johannine community, according to tradition, was centered in Ephesus; the seven churches of Revelation include the Ephesian church as first-addressed).
8: He entered the synagogue and for three months spoke boldly (parrēsiazomenos), reasoning and persuading about the kingdom of God (peri tēs basileias tou theou): three months of synagogue engagement before expulsion is the longest synagogue run in Acts (cf. three Sabbaths in Thessalonica, Acts 17:2). The 'kingdom of God' as the topic — Paul's preaching in Acts consistently centers on the kingdom (Acts 28:23, 31) — reflects the continuity between Jesus's proclamation and the apostolic message. The Ephesian community's extended receptivity made Ephesus Paul's most productive base.
9: When some became stubborn and continued in unbelief, speaking evil of the Way before the congregation, he withdrew from them and took the disciples with him, reasoning daily in the hall of Tyrannus (tē scholē Tyrannōu): scholē is the Greek word for a school — a lecture hall or space used for philosophical teaching. Tyrannus (possibly a local philosopher or teacher who rented out space) provided the venue Paul used for daily instruction. One Western text adds 'from the fifth to the tenth hour' (11am-4pm) — the midday hours when schools were typically idle, Paul using the space during its off-peak period.
10: This continued for two years, so that all the residents of Asia heard the word of the Lord, both Jews and Greeks (pantas tous katoikountas tēn Asian akousai ton logon tou kyriou): the two years of daily teaching in the Tyrannean hall produced a regional missionary effect: the seven churches of Revelation (Smyrna, Pergamum, Thyatira, Sardis, Philadelphia, Laodicea — all in Asia province) were probably founded during or as a result of this period, as were Colossae and Hierapolis (Colossians 1:2, 7 implies Epaphras evangelized Colossae, presumably trained or deployed from Ephesus).
11: God was doing extraordinary miracles (dynameis ou tas tychousas) by the hands of Paul: ou tas tychousas (not ordinary/not the common sort) is litotes — these were unusual even by the standard of apostolic signs. The textual description — handkerchiefs and aprons from Paul's body being carried to the sick — reflects the ancient belief in the transferability of a holy person's power through physical contact (cf. the woman with hemorrhage touching Jesus's garment, Luke 8:44; Peter's shadow healing, Acts 5:15).
12: Handkerchiefs (soudaria) or aprons (simikinthia) that had touched his skin were carried away to the sick (apopheresthai apo tou chrōtos autou soudaria ē simikinthia kai apallassesthai ap autōn tas nosous): soudaria is the Latinism for sweat-cloths (face-cloths); simikinthia are workman's aprons — consistent with Paul's tentmaking practice. The healing through contact-objects created a demand for Paul's personal effects as healing media, which became the occasion for the exorcist fraud of Acts 19:13-16.
13: Some itinerant Jewish exorcists (tines tōn perierchomenōn Ioudaiōn exorkistōn): Jewish professional exorcists were attested in antiquity (Josephus, Ant. 8.45-47 — describes exorcistic techniques attributed to Solomon; the Testament of Solomon is an elaborate magical manual). The Ephesus magical papyri (Papyri Graecae Magicae) document the rich magical tradition of the city. These exorcists attempted to appropriate Paul's Jesus-name formula as a more powerful tool — treating the name of Jesus as a ritual invocation like other names of power in magical practice.
14: Seven sons of Sceva, a Jewish chief priest (archiereōs Ioudaiou), were doing this: 'chief priest' may be an informal title for a senior priestly family rather than the actual High Priest (no Sceva is known from high-priestly lists). Seven sons practicing exorcism together is a business arrangement — the family exorcism trade. Their attempt to use the formula 'I adjure you by the Jesus whom Paul proclaims' reveals that they recognize Paul's authority as derived from Jesus without understanding what that authority actually entails.
15: 'Jesus I know, and Paul I recognize, but who are you?' (ton men Iēsoun ginōskō kai ton Paulon epistamai, hymeis de tines este): the evil spirit's response makes the theological point with brutal comedy: genuine spiritual authority is personal and relational, not formulaic and transferable. The demon acknowledges Jesus and Paul as known quantities — their authority is authentic. The exorcists' lack of this relationship makes their formula empty; they are strangers to the power they are trying to invoke.
16: The man in whom was the evil spirit leaped on them, overpowered them all, and escaped from that house naked and wounded (gymnus kai tetFaumatismenous ekphugoIn ek tou oikou ekeinou): 'all' of them (amphoteron in some texts, suggesting two; pantōn in others, suggesting all seven) — overpowered by a single possessed man. The physical humiliation of the exorcists — stripped and wounded, fleeing — provides vivid evidence that the Jesus-name is not a magic formula but a personal authority. The narrative would have circulated rapidly in Ephesus's magical-practitioner community.
17: This became known to all the residents of Ephesus, Jews and Greeks, and fear fell on them all (epepesen phobos epi pantas autous) and the name of the Lord Jesus was extolled (emegalyneto to onoma tou kyriou Iēsou): the community's response connects fear and honor. The episode distinguishes authentic from fraudulent use of Jesus's name precisely by the failure of the fraudulent attempt; the name's power is vindicated by its non-transferability to unauthorized users. This becomes the occasion for the community's self-purification in Acts 19:18-20.
18: Many of those who were now believers came confessing and divulging their practices (exaggellontes tas praxeis autōn): the confessional disclosure of magical practices by new converts reflects the community's understanding that belonging to Christ required renunciation of the magical world. The confession is corporate — 'many who were now believers' had apparently maintained dual practice after initial conversion. The Sceva episode is the trigger for genuine repentance that purges this syncretism.
19: A number of those who had practiced magic arts brought their books together and burned them in the sight of all (katekaiOn enōpion pantōn), and they calculated the value of them and found it came to fifty thousand pieces of silver (drachmas myriadas pente): fifty thousand drachmas was approximately fifty thousand days' wages for a laborer — an enormous sum. The Ephesian magical papyri found by archaeologists give a sense of the genre: lengthy, expensive texts combining Jewish, Egyptian, and Greek divine names in complex ritual formulae. Their public burning signaled complete repudiation of the magical system.
20: So the word of the Lord continued to increase and prevail mightily (houtōs kata kratos tou kyriou ho logos ēuxanen kai ischyen): the summary formula marks the completion of the Ephesian mission's first phase and closes Acts' third major sequence. Kata kratos (with force/mightily) is the strongest growth formula in Acts' six summary statements (Acts 6:7; Acts 9:31; Acts 12:24; Acts 16:5; Acts 19:20; Acts 28:31). The burning of the magical books — renunciation of the rival spiritual system — is the soil from which the mighty growth emerges.
21: After these events Paul resolved in the Spirit (etheto en tō pneumati ho Paulos) to pass through Macedonia and Achaia and go to Jerusalem, saying, 'After I have been there, I must also see Rome' (dei me kai Rōmēn idein): the itinerary planning moment is both strategic and pneumatic — 'resolved in the Spirit' is a Lukan phrase for Spirit-guided decision. The Rome vision is the first in Acts; 23:11 will confirm it as divine purpose: 'you must testify also in Rome'. Paul's letter to Rome (Romans, written from Corinth ca. 57 CE) is the theological preparation for a visit he plans as a missionary base for Spain (Romans 15:24-28).
22: He sent into Macedonia two of his helpers, Timothy and Erastus (Timotheou kai Eraston), while he himself stayed in Asia for a while: Timothy is Paul's established co-worker since the second journey; Erastus appears in Romans 16:23 as 'the city treasurer' of Corinth, suggesting a person of social standing who travels with Paul. The advance party to Macedonia prepares for Paul's own journey (Acts 20:1-2) through the churches there. The two-person sending pattern reflects both Jewish testimony requirements and practical travel security.
24: Demetrius, a silversmith who made silver shrines of Artemis, brought no little business to the craftsmen (pareicheto tois technItais ouk oligēn ergasian): the Artemis cult was Ephesus's primary industry — the temple was a tourist destination, treasury, and civic identity center. Silver miniature temples (naous argyrous) were votive offerings and souvenirs; their production was a major economic sector. Demetrius represents the guild leadership of a craft industry whose livelihood was directly threatened by the gospel's advance (cf. Acts 16:16-19 — the fortune-telling slave in Philippi).
25: 'Men, you know that from this business we have our wealth' (ek tautēs tēs ergasias hē euporia hēmin estin): Demetrius's speech frankly acknowledges the economic motivation alongside the religious one. The guild meeting (summoning craftsmen and related workers) is a collegium gathering — the professional trade associations of the Roman world had religious, social, and economic functions bound together. The appeal to economic interest is the politically honest core of the riot's instigation, though the religious language (Acts 19:27) provides its public legitimacy.
26: 'This Paul has persuaded and turned away a great many people, saying that gods made with hands are not gods' (legōn hoti ouk eisin theoi hoi dia cheirōn ginomenoi): the anti-idol formulation echoes the prophetic polemic (Isaiah 44:9-20; Jeremiah 10:3-5; Psalms 115:4-8). Paul's proclamation in Ephesus apparently included direct critique of idol manufacture — not merely positive proclamation but negative argument against the theological category that made Demetrius's business meaningful. The 'not only in Ephesus but in almost all of Asia' spread shows the regional impact of the Ephesian mission.
27: 'Not only is there danger that this trade of ours may come into disrepute (eis apelegmon elthein), but also that the temple of the great goddess Artemis may be counted as nothing' (eis outhen logisthēnai): the Artemis temple was more than a religious site — it was the greatest bank in western Anatolia (holding deposits from cities and kings), a major employer through tourism and craft industries, and the supreme symbol of Ephesian civic identity. The threat to Artemis's honor was a threat to the city's self-understanding; Demetrius's appeal to civic pride is more powerful than his appeal to economic interest.
28: When they heard this they were enraged and were crying out, 'Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!' (megalē hē Artemis Ephesiōn): the cultic acclamation was a standard liturgical shout — inscriptions from Ephesus preserve this exact formula as a temple dedication. The crowd's eruption from a guild meeting into a city-wide riot shows how quickly artisanal economic grievance could mobilize civic religious identity. The theater (seating ~24,000) was the venue for civic assemblies (ekklēsia) — appropriate for the crisis that the city's identity is under threat.
29: The city was filled with the confusion (synchuseōs) and they rushed together into the theater, dragging with them Gaius and Aristarchus, Macedonians who were Paul's travel companions: Gaius and Aristarchus (Colossians 4:10 identifies Aristarchus as a Thessalonian co-prisoner of Paul) are seized as surrogates when Paul himself cannot be found or is protected. The theater was the civic assembly space of Ephesus — a political ekklēsia turned into a mob. Luke's use of ekklēsia for both the Christian assembly and this civic gathering (Acts 19:39) is probably intentional contrast.
30: Paul wished to go in to the crowd (boulomenou de tou Paulou eiselthein eis ton dēmon), but the disciples would not let him (ouk eiōn auton hoi mathētai): Paul's impulse to address the crowd directly is characteristic — his boldness in hostile situations (Acts 9:29; Acts 22:1) would put him at serious personal risk in an anti-Paul mob. The disciples' restraint is the community's protective care for its founder. Even 'some of the Asiarchs who were his friends sent to him and were urging him not to venture into the theater' — a remarkable detail showing Paul had cultivated relationships with senior Roman-appointed provincial officials.
31: Some of the Asiarchs (Asiarchai) who were his friends sent to him: the Asiarchs were the leading civic officials of the Asian province — wealthy, romanized provincials who presided over the imperial cult and provincial games. Their friendship with Paul shows that his Ephesian mission had reached the highest social levels of the city, not just the artisan and synagogue communities. The Asiarchs' warning reflects both personal concern and their own interest in preventing disorder that could embarrass the province before Rome.
32: Now some cried out one thing, some another, for the assembly was in confusion (en sugkechymenē hē ekklēsia), and most of them did not know why they had come together: the theater's chaotic condition — most participants not knowing what the gathering was about — is realistic. Ancient mob scenes were frequently characterized by contagious energy without coherent purpose. Luke's detail about the confusion is both realistic and ironic: the 'great is Artemis' crowd is as confused as any disorganized mob.
33: Some of the crowd prompted Alexander, whom the Jews had put forward (probAlantown ton Alexandron hoi Ioudaioi): the Jewish community's attempt to distance themselves from Paul (by putting forward a spokesman to disclaim connection) shows the diaspora Jews' vulnerability — they could be targeted alongside the Christians by an anti-monotheist crowd. Alexander's attempt to speak is silenced by the crowd's recognition that he was a Jew; the anti-idol stance of Judaism made the Jewish community a secondary target of Artemis-cult loyalty.
34: For about two hours they kept shouting, 'Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!' (megalē hē Artemis Ephesiōn): two hours of chanting the cultic acclamation is historically credible — ancient civic assemblies and mob gatherings could sustain rhythmic chanting for extended periods. The shout simultaneously expressed religious loyalty, civic identity, economic anxiety, and social bonding. Archaeological inscriptions confirm the formula; Luke's documentation of it is consistent with the epigraphic record.
35: The town clerk (grammateus) quieted the crowd and said, 'Men of Ephesus, who is there who does not know that the city of the Ephesians is temple keeper (neōkoron) of the great Artemis and of the sacred stone that fell from the sky?': the grammateus was the most important civic official in a Greek city — the secretary who managed public records and communications with the Roman governor. His intervention is the legal voice of civic order. Neōkoron (temple warden) was Ephesus's official title, confirmed by coins and inscriptions — it was the city's proudest identity marker. The sacred stone (diopetous, fell from Zeus) may refer to a meteorite housed in the temple.
36: 'Seeing then that these things cannot be denied, you ought to be quiet (katestellenous hyparkhein hymas ēsychazein dei) and do nothing rash' — the town clerk's appeal to the unquestionable nature of Artemis's greatness is the rhetorical strategy of dismissing the threat to neutralize the riot. If Artemis's greatness cannot be challenged, then the Christians' preaching poses no real threat worth rioting about. The argument is political logic: overreacting to a minor religious nuisance creates more danger (Roman intervention) than the nuisance itself.
37: 'You have brought these men here who are neither temple robbers (hierosylous) nor blasphemers of our goddess' (blasphēmountas tēn theon hēmōn): the town clerk's legal verdict is the fourth such exoneration in Acts' judicial sequence (cf. Gallio's ruling, Acts 18:14-15; Festus, Acts 25:18-19; Agrippa, Acts 26:31-32). Luke's consistent documentation of Roman and civic officials declaring the Christians innocent of actionable offenses is his sustained defense of Christianity's legal legitimacy. The specific charges — temple robbery and blasphemy — are the most serious that could be brought in an Artemis-city context.
38: If Demetrius and the craftsmen have a complaint against anyone, the courts are open (agoraioi agontai kai anthupatoi eisin) and there are proconsuls — let them bring charges against one another: the town clerk redirects the guild's complaint to the proper legal channel — the courts and proconsuls of the Roman province. The appeal to proper legal procedure is both civic wisdom and political self-protection: if the riot leads to Roman intervention, Ephesian civic officials will be held accountable. The reference to proconsuls (plural) may be an error or reflect multiple deputies.
39: If you seek anything further, it shall be settled in the regular assembly (en tē ennomō ekklēsia, the lawful/regular assembly): the contrast between the current ekklēsia (mob assembly) and the ennomos ekklēsia (legally constituted assembly) distinguishes legitimate civic deliberation from riot. The regular assembly met three times a month in Ephesus by constitutional prescription; its decisions had legal standing. The town clerk is insisting that civic governance requires proper procedure — the mob has no legal authority to decide anything.
40: 'For we really are in danger of being charged with rioting today (enstaseos peri tēs sēmeron egkaleisthai staseōs), there being no cause that we can give to justify this commotion' — the town clerk's concern about Roman investigation is the political clincher. Provincial cities that generated riots came under Roman scrutiny; civic officials who failed to maintain order could face sanctions. His intervention is ultimately self-protective civic management, but the providential effect is Paul's protection. The Roman administrative system again unwittingly serves the mission's preservation.
41: And when he had said these things, he dismissed the assembly (apelysen tēn ekklēsian): the town clerk's formal dismissal restores civic order. Luke's narrative of the Ephesian riot is the most detailed civic disturbance scene in Acts — richer in specific detail than even the Jerusalem crowd scenes. Its resolution through civic procedure rather than Roman military intervention or apostolic miracle is Luke's pointed demonstration that the gospel does not require special pleading or divine rescue from every crisis; ordinary civil order, rightly invoked, can protect the mission.
